item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Has anyone written an explainer for this one, or done the research for it? I started comparing the reactions to the shadow with the Caesar Incident investigation roles in Kirby's 001, but… I dunno, nothing immediately obvious jumped out at me. I'm hoping to at least hear from somebody that the payoff for doing the forensic legwork is worth the effort.

The format screw seems kind of arbitrary. I can't figure out why

+ spoilers
- spoilers

"the logo doesn't appear in the scip webpage header"


is relevant to "an anomalous shadow of the logo appears on the wall" (beyond both of those involving the logo).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Take a look in the top-left corner. Notice anything missing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the idea of a shadow of the SCP symbol we all love. And the theories behind this one are fascinating. Perhaps the different reactions are the suppressed reactions towards the guilt they have for what they have caused?

Nonetheless, as Rock stated, I feel as though the SCP was not investigated as well as it should have been. As a SCP that only affects some of the most important people in the foundation it kind of concerns me that it would be just brushed off as it has been.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel as if this could have been better if the characters didn't investigate the anomaly as much as they do. I understand that there is cause for alarm… initially. Because it's an anomaly in their presence, yes, but it's a rather tame anomaly that doesn't have any apparent lasting effect on the O5. I'm not sure what the initial draft was, but I'm thinking ARD has it right that having the O5 be dismissive of the anomaly's effects and keep it out of their sight. I think that with the combination of the "dehumanizing" commentary would make for a stronger impact. As this is right now, I personally find it kind of dull.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm all for explorations of Overseers and their personal existences, but this doesn't really bring anything new to the table. I feel like everything brought up in this piece has been written about in more depth elsewhere, especially this exchange:

O5-13: No, not like that. Think about it. Sitting here, making decisions every day that result in people dying, while we sit safely tucked away miles from any danger? It dehumanizes us.

O5-1: No one forced your hand to accept this role.

O5-13: It's just… take today. I've just signed an order authorizing the procurement of forty new D-Class Personnel from twelve American prisons. I won't ever see them die, or have to feed and shelter them, they're letters and figures on a page for me. When was the last time you were within spitting distance of an anomaly, not counting [REDACTED]?

The dehumanizing element seems so inherent in the nature of the Council that bringing it up but not exploring any further is essentially saying nothing at all. If this piece isn't not going to examine the issue, nor particularly develop the characters, I'm not sure what it's trying to accomplish from a storytelling perspective.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The attempts at lampshading, ironically enough, don't throw enough shade on the base concept. You're basically just rattling off entities, fake units of measurement, and creating a dialogue that tells us to accept the thing at face value. The punchline has also been neutered; I much preferred the original one where O5-1 pragmatically says to keep the room lights on. It was funnier and sharper.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kind of like this? I'm a sucker for light vs. dark imagery and commentary on the Overseer Council, so this should be right up my alley. There's a lot of stuff to unpack here — a lot of meaning and metaphor fo discover — but I can't help but feel like an extended dialogue isn't the way to reveal that. I felt like my hand was being held, I guess.

This is interesting af and super well-written, but I think I'm gonna novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
“Some people seemed to get all sunshine, and some all shadow…”

An article about the O5 Council's looming shadow.

Thanks to Conwell, Lt flops, Popsioak, A Random Day, Mew-ltwoverse, rattles, and everyone else who reviewed this.

While you're here, you may as well check out some of my other works.
